events.text: Patient is a 52-year-old African American male with a past medical history of well-controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension who presented with progressive fatigue, night sweats, and left upper quadrant fullness.
At age 52, the patient was diagnosed with acute myeloid leukemia NOS, ICD-O morphology code 9861/3, primary site bone marrow C42.1, after workup for splenomegaly and circulating blasts.

At age 52, initial laboratory evaluation showed white blood cell count of 28,000/uL with 44% blasts, hemoglobin 8.8 g/dL, and platelet count of 56,000/uL. LDH was 780 U/L. HbA1c was 7.2% and renal function was normal with creatinine 0.9 mg/dL. Coagulation studies were within normal limits.
At age 52, abdominal ultrasound demonstrated splenomegaly measuring 18.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension. No hepatomegaly or lymphadenopathy was identified.

At age 52, bone marrow biopsy with aspirate showed a hypercellular marrow (90% cellularity) with 58% myeloblasts. Blasts were intermediate in size with moderate cytoplasm and round to oval nuclei with fine chromatin. Flow cytometry immunophenotype was positive for CD34, CD117, CD33, CD13, HLA-DR, and MPO with aberrant co-expression of CD7.

At age 52, next-generation sequencing identified FLT3-ITD mutation with allelic ratio 0.86, DNMT3A R882H mutation, and RUNX1 frameshift mutation at exon 8. NPM1 was wild-type. TP53 and ASXL1 were wild-type.

At age 52, cytogenetic analysis revealed translocation t(6;9)(p23;q34.1) identified in 16 of 20 metaphases. FISH confirmed DEK-NUP214 fusion. ELN 2022 risk was adverse based on t(6;9) and FLT3-ITD without NPM1 mutation.
At age 52, induction therapy was initiated with 7+3 cytarabine 200 mg/m2 continuous infusion and daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 combined with midostaurin 50 mg twice daily on days eight through twenty-one.

At age 52, the patient developed neutropenic fever on day six with absolute neutrophil count of 0. CT chest showed a peripheral nodule with surrounding halo sign in the left upper lobe concerning for angioinvasive aspergillosis. Serum galactomannan was positive at 2.8 index and voriconazole was initiated.
At age 52, hyperglycemia was difficult to control during induction requiring insulin drip and later transition to a basal-bolus insulin regimen with total daily dose of 86 units.
At age 52, day 14 bone marrow biopsy showed a hypocellular marrow (20% cellularity) with 6% residual blasts, an indeterminate result requiring continued observation.

At age 52, day 28 bone marrow biopsy demonstrated 2.8% blasts by morphology consistent with complete remission, but flow cytometry MRD was positive at 0.18%. Cytogenetics showed the t(6;9) was no longer detectable.

At age 52, given adverse risk features and MRD positivity, the patient was expedited to allogeneic stem cell transplant. He received one cycle of consolidation HiDAC 3 g/m2 plus midostaurin while a donor search was conducted.

At age 52, the patient underwent allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant from an eight-of-eight HLA-matched unrelated donor with myeloablative conditioning consisting of total body irradiation 1,200 cGy in six fractions and cyclophosphamide 60 mg/kg for two days.
At age 52, GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus and mycophenolate mofetil. Neutrophil engraftment occurred on day plus 16.

At age 52, the patient developed sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (hepatic veno-occlusive disease) on day plus 12 with painful hepatomegaly, weight gain of six kilograms, and bilirubin rising to 8.4 mg/dL, treated with defibrotide.
At age 52, grade 1 acute GVHD of the skin developed with maculopapular rash over less than 25% body surface area, managed with topical corticosteroids alone.
At age 52, day plus 100 bone marrow showed complete donor chimerism at 98.8% with no morphologic or flow cytometric evidence of residual leukemia. FLT3-ITD was undetectable by PCR.
At age 53, at one year post-transplant the patient remained in complete remission with full donor chimerism and was successfully tapered off all immunosuppression without flare of GVHD.
At age 53, PET-CT at one year post-transplant demonstrated no FDG-avid disease. Previously noted pulmonary nodule had resolved completely. Spleen had returned to normal size at 11.4 cm.

scenario_blurb: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with FLT3-ITD mutation. ELN intermediate/adverse risk. Induction with 7+3 plus midostaurin. Some achieving CR, some refractory. Include cytogenetics (normal karyotype or complex), blast percentage monitoring, allogeneic stem cell transplant in first remission for eligible patients.
